source_story: As a Visitor, I want to sign up via github or google, so that that I don't have to enter lots of information and remember my password for yet another website.
rewritten_story: As a Visitor, I want to register using github or google, so that I can avoid filling in a lot of details and having to remember another website password.
rationale: The rewritten story keeps the same role, sign-up options, and benefit while expressing the requirement in different wording.

source_story: As a Visitor, I want to sign up via github or google, so that that I don't have to enter lots of information and remember my password for yet another website.
rewritten_story: As a security administrator, I want visitors to be prevented from signing up with GitHub or Google and required to create accounts using our verified email registration process, so that account access follows our approved identity policy.
rationale: The generated story keeps the same sign-up and identity-provider topic but changes the access policy to disallow GitHub or Google sign-up, which is materially incompatible with the source story allowing visitors to use those providers.

source_story: As a Visitor, I want to sign up via github or google, so that that I don't have to enter lots of information and remember my password for yet another website.
rewritten_story: As a Visitor, I want to sign up without using GitHub or Google, so that I can create an account even when I do not want to use a social login provider.
rationale: The generated story keeps the same visitor sign-up feature area but changes the central sign-up condition from using GitHub or Google to not using those providers, making the two stories materially incompatible as stated.

source_story: As a Visitor, I want to sign up via github or google, so that that I don't have to enter lots of information and remember my password for yet another website.
rewritten_story: As an administrator, I want to configure GitHub and Google as approved sign-up providers so that visitors can use those accounts to register for the website.
rationale: The generated story establishes the required provider configuration that must exist before visitors can sign up using GitHub or Google.

source_story: As a Publisher, I want to know what do next after signing up, so that that I can get going quickly.
rewritten_story: As a Publisher, I want clear guidance on the next steps after I sign up, so that I can get started quickly.
rationale: The rewritten story keeps the same role, post-signup guidance need, and benefit of getting started quickly while using different wording.

source_story: As a Publisher, I want to know what do next after signing up, so that that I can get going quickly.
rewritten_story: As a Publisher, I want the next-step onboarding guidance to be accessible only after my publisher account has been approved, so that unapproved signups cannot begin setup prematurely.
rationale: The generated story keeps the post-signup publisher onboarding topic but changes the governing access rule by requiring account approval before next-step guidance is available, which is materially incompatible with immediate guidance after signing up.

source_story: As a Publisher, I want to know what do next after signing up, so that that I can get going quickly.
rewritten_story: As a Publisher, I want to know what to do next before signing up, so that I can understand how to get started quickly.
rationale: The generated story keeps the onboarding guidance goal for Publishers but changes the central condition from after signing up to before signing up, making the applicable account state mutually incompatible while staying in the same backlog area.

source_story: As a Publisher, I want to know what do next after signing up, so that that I can get going quickly.
rewritten_story: As a Publisher, I want to verify my email and activate my publisher account so that I can access the onboarding guidance for my next steps.
rationale: The generated story establishes account activation as a necessary prior condition for accessing the post-sign-up next-step guidance described in the source story.

source_story: As an Admin, I want to invite someone to join the platform, so that that they can start contributing or using data.
rewritten_story: As an Admin, I want to send an invitation for someone to join the platform, so they can begin contributing or working with data.
rationale: The rewritten story keeps the same Admin role, invitation capability, platform target, and intended benefit while expressing the requirement with different wording.

source_story: As an Admin, I want to invite someone to join the platform, so that that they can start contributing or using data.
rewritten_story: As an Admin, I want invitations to join the platform to be sent only after an Organization Owner approves them, so that access is controlled before new users can contribute or use data.
rationale: The generated story keeps the same invitation workflow but changes the governing access rule from Admins being able to invite directly to requiring Organization Owner approval before an invitation can be sent.

source_story: As an Admin, I want to invite someone to join the platform, so that that they can start contributing or using data.
rewritten_story: As an Admin, I want to invite someone to join the platform only when their account is inactive, so that they can start contributing or using data after reactivation.
rationale: The generated story keeps the same invitation capability but changes the account state under which inviting is allowed, from a general case to the inactive-account case, creating a materially different applicability condition.

source_story: As an Admin, I want to invite someone to join the platform, so that that they can start contributing or using data.
rewritten_story: As an Admin, I want to configure the available user roles and data access groups so that new invitees can be assigned the right permissions when they join the platform.
rationale: The generated story establishes the roles and access options needed before an admin can invite someone who will contribute or use data on the platform.

source_story: As a Consumer, I want to see how much the data has been downloaded, so that that I can choose most popular in the case when there are several alternatives for my use case.
rewritten_story: As a Consumer, I want to view how often the data has been downloaded, so that I can select the most popular option when multiple alternatives fit my use case.
rationale: The rewritten story keeps the same role, need to see download popularity, and benefit of choosing the most popular alternative, while expressing it in clearer different wording.

source_story: As a Consumer, I want to see how much the data has been downloaded, so that that I can choose most popular in the case when there are several alternatives for my use case.
rewritten_story: As a marketplace administrator, I want dataset download counts to be visible only to data owners and admins, so that consumers cannot rank alternatives based on popularity alone.
rationale: The generated story keeps the same dataset download-count feature area but introduces an incompatible access-control rule that prevents consumers from seeing the metric requested in the source story.

source_story: As a Consumer, I want to see how much the data has been downloaded, so that that I can choose most popular in the case when there are several alternatives for my use case.
rewritten_story: As a Consumer, I want to see how much the data has been downloaded only for hidden datasets, so that I can compare interest before they are made public.
rationale: The generated story keeps the same download-visibility capability but changes the dataset visibility state under which the information is shown, from a general case to the hidden-dataset case, creating a materially different applicability condition.

source_story: As a Consumer, I want to see how much the data has been downloaded, so that that I can choose most popular in the case when there are several alternatives for my use case.
rewritten_story: As a Platform Administrator, I want each dataset download to be recorded and linked to the correct dataset, so that reliable download counts are available for consumers.
rationale: The generated story establishes the necessary tracking and data availability needed before consumers can view how much each dataset has been downloaded.

source_story: As a Publisher, I want to see real examples of published packages so that I can understand how useful and simple the datapackage format and the registry is.
rewritten_story: As a Publisher, I want to view actual examples of published packages so that I can understand how useful and easy to use the datapackage format and the registry are.
rationale: The rewritten story keeps the same role, objective, target examples, and benefit while expressing the requirement in different wording.

source_story: As a Publisher, I want to see real examples of published packages so that I can understand how useful and simple the datapackage format and the registry is.
rewritten_story: As a Registry administrator, I want real examples of published packages to be visible only to approved registry reviewers so that publisher content is not exposed to general Publishers without permission.
rationale: The generated story stays in the same area of published package examples but introduces an incompatible access-control rule: Publishers can no longer freely view the examples; only approved registry reviewers may access them.

source_story: As a Publisher, I want to see real examples of published packages so that I can understand how useful and simple the datapackage format and the registry is.
rewritten_story: As a Publisher, I want to see real examples of draft packages so that I can understand how useful and simple the datapackage format and the registry is before publication.
rationale: The generated story keeps the same publisher role, example-viewing capability, and datapackage registry context, but changes the package state from published to draft, making the condition under which the examples apply materially incompatible.

source_story: As a Publisher, I want to see real examples of published packages so that I can understand how useful and simple the datapackage format and the registry is.
rewritten_story: As a registry administrator, I want to seed the registry with approved public datapackage examples so that publishers have real published packages available to browse.
rationale: The generated story establishes the required prior availability of real published package examples in the registry, which is necessary before a publisher can view them as described in the source story.

source_story: As a Consumer, I want to download the data package in one file, so that that I don't have to download descriptor and each resource by hand.
rewritten_story: As a Consumer, I want to get the full data package as a single file, so that I do not need to manually download the descriptor and every resource separately.
rationale: The rewritten story keeps the same role, objective, target data package, and benefit while expressing the requirement with different wording.

source_story: As a Consumer, I want to download the data package in one file, so that that I don't have to download descriptor and each resource by hand.
rewritten_story: As a Data Owner, I want the one-file data package download to be available only to approved internal users, so that consumers cannot bypass the required per-resource access controls.
rationale: The generated story keeps the same data package download feature but changes the governing access policy by restricting bundled downloads to approved internal users, which conflicts with allowing consumers to download the full package in one file.

source_story: As a Consumer, I want to download the data package in one file, so that that I don't have to download descriptor and each resource by hand.
rewritten_story: As a Consumer, I want to download the data package as separate descriptor and resource files, so that I can work with each component individually.
rationale: The generated story keeps the same data package download feature area but changes the package condition from bundled into one file to split into separate descriptor and resource files, making the two stories materially incompatible.

source_story: As a Consumer, I want to download the data package in one file, so that that I don't have to download descriptor and each resource by hand.
rewritten_story: As a Data Provider, I want to publish a data package with its descriptor and all associated resources included, so that consumers have a complete package available for single-file download.
rationale: The generated story establishes the necessary prior condition that the complete data package, including descriptor and resources, is published and available before a consumer can download it as one file.

source_story: As a Developer, I want to use data package as a node lib in my project, so that that I can depend on it using my normal dependency framework.
rewritten_story: As a Developer, I want to include the data package as a Node library in my project, so that I can manage it with my normal dependency framework.
rationale: The rewritten story keeps the same role, objective, target package, and dependency-management benefit while expressing the requirement in different wording.

source_story: As a Developer, I want to use data package as a node lib in my project, so that that I can depend on it using my normal dependency framework.
rewritten_story: As a platform administrator, I want the data package Node library to be installable only by approved internal projects, so that access to the package is controlled through our dependency registry.
rationale: The generated story keeps the same data package and Node dependency context but changes the access policy from any developer using it in their project to only approved internal projects being allowed to install it.

source_story: As a Developer, I want to use data package as a node lib in my project, so that that I can depend on it using my normal dependency framework.
rewritten_story: As a Developer, I want to use the data package in my project when it is not packaged as a Node lib, so that I can still manage it through my normal dependency workflow.
rationale: The generated story keeps the same developer dependency-management context and target data package, but changes the central package condition from being available as a Node library to not being packaged as a Node library, making the two stories materially incompatible as stated.

source_story: As a Developer, I want to use data package as a node lib in my project, so that that I can depend on it using my normal dependency framework.
rewritten_story: As a Developer, I want the data package to be published as a versioned Node package in the project package registry, so that I can install it through my normal dependency framework.
rationale: The generated story establishes the required package availability and versioning needed before a developer can use the data package as a Node library dependency.

source_story: As a Consumer, I want to load a Data Package from R, so that that I can immediately start playing with it.
rewritten_story: As a Consumer, I want to import a Data Package in R so that I can start exploring it right away.
rationale: The rewritten story keeps the same role, Data Package objective, R context, and immediate-use benefit while expressing the need in different wording.

source_story: As a Consumer, I want to load a Data Package from R, so that that I can immediately start playing with it.
rewritten_story: As a Data Package owner, I want Consumers to be able to load my Data Package from R only after I have granted them access, so that restricted packages cannot be opened without permission.
rationale: The generated story keeps the same Data Package loading from R feature area but introduces an incompatible access-control rule requiring owner-granted permission before a Consumer may load the package.

source_story: As a Consumer, I want to load a Data Package from R, so that that I can immediately start playing with it.
rewritten_story: As a Consumer, I want to load a Data Package outside of R, so that I can immediately start playing with it in a non-R environment.
rationale: The generated story keeps the same goal of loading a Data Package for immediate exploration but changes the runtime condition from using R to operating outside of R, making the two stories incompatible in the environment where the capability applies.

source_story: As a Consumer, I want to load a Data Package from R, so that that I can immediately start playing with it.
rewritten_story: As a Consumer, I want to install an R library that supports Data Packages, so that my R environment is ready to open them.
rationale: The generated story establishes the required R environment capability needed before a consumer can load a Data Package from R as described in the source story.

source_story: As a Consumer, I want to download a DataPackage's data one coherent SQLite database, so that that I can get it easily in one form.
rewritten_story: As a Consumer, I want to download a DataPackage's data as a single coherent SQLite database so that I can access it easily in one format.
rationale: The rewritten story keeps the same role, objective, target data package, SQLite database format, and ease-of-access benefit while rephrasing the requirement.

source_story: As a Consumer, I want to download a DataPackage's data one coherent SQLite database, so that that I can get it easily in one form.
rewritten_story: As a DataPackage owner, I want downloads of a DataPackage as one coherent SQLite database to be restricted to approved users only, so that unauthorized consumers cannot access the full dataset in one file.
rationale: The generated story keeps the same DataPackage SQLite download feature but changes the access-control rule from consumer availability to owner-controlled approved-user access, making it materially incompatible with the source permission expectation.

source_story: As a Consumer, I want to download a DataPackage's data one coherent SQLite database, so that that I can get it easily in one form.
rewritten_story: As a Consumer, I want to download a DataPackage's data as separate SQLite databases for each resource, so that I can handle each part independently.
rationale: The generated story keeps the same consumer download feature for DataPackage data in SQLite form, but changes the central state of the downloaded artifact from one coherent database to separate databases, making the two requirements incompatible as stated.

source_story: As a Consumer, I want to download a DataPackage's data one coherent SQLite database, so that that I can get it easily in one form.
rewritten_story: As a DataPackage maintainer, I want to publish the package with validated data resources and metadata so that it can be prepared as one coherent SQLite database for consumers.
rationale: The generated story establishes the necessary prior condition that the DataPackage's resources and metadata are available and valid before a consumer can download them as a coherent SQLite database.

source_story: As a Data Analyst, I want to compare different versions of some datapackage locally, so that that I can see schema changes clearly and adjust my analytics code to the desired schema version.
rewritten_story: As a Data Analyst, I want to locally compare different versions of a datapackage so that I can clearly identify schema changes and adapt my analytics code to the schema version I need.
rationale: The rewritten story keeps the same role, local datapackage version comparison objective, and benefit of understanding schema changes to adjust analytics code, while expressing the requirement with different wording.

source_story: As a Data Analyst, I want to compare different versions of some datapackage locally, so that that I can see schema changes clearly and adjust my analytics code to the desired schema version.
rewritten_story: As a Data Steward, I want local comparison of datapackage versions to be available only to the datapackage owner or analysts with explicit steward approval, so that schema changes are not exposed to unauthorized users.
rationale: The generated story stays in the datapackage version comparison area but introduces an incompatible access-control rule requiring ownership or steward approval, rather than allowing a data analyst to compare datapackage versions locally by default.

source_story: As a Data Analyst, I want to compare different versions of some datapackage locally, so that that I can see schema changes clearly and adjust my analytics code to the desired schema version.
rewritten_story: As a Data Analyst, I want to compare different versions of some datapackage in the remote registry, so that I can see schema changes clearly and adjust my analytics code to the desired schema version.
rationale: The generated story keeps the same datapackage version comparison capability and analytics workflow, but changes the central runtime condition from comparing locally to comparing in a remote registry, making the two stories materially incompatible as stated.

source_story: As a Data Analyst, I want to compare different versions of some datapackage locally, so that that I can see schema changes clearly and adjust my analytics code to the desired schema version.
rewritten_story: As a Data Analyst, I want to download and keep selected versions of a datapackage with their version metadata locally, so that the versions are available for schema review and analysis work.
rationale: The generated story establishes the necessary local availability of the datapackage versions and their metadata before the analyst can compare those versions locally.

source_story: As a Web Developer, I want to be able to install multiple versions of the same datapackage separately, so that that all my projects could be developed independently and deployed locally.
rewritten_story: As a Web Developer, I want to install different versions of the same datapackage side by side, so that each of my projects can be developed independently and deployed locally.
rationale: The rewritten story keeps the same role, objective, target datapackage versioning need, and benefit while expressing the requirement in different wording.

source_story: As a Web Developer, I want to be able to install multiple versions of the same datapackage separately, so that that all my projects could be developed independently and deployed locally.
rewritten_story: As a Web Developer, I want each datapackage to be installable only in the single version approved for the local workspace, so that projects cannot use separate unapproved versions of the same datapackage.
rationale: The generated story stays in the datapackage installation and versioning area but introduces an incompatible access-control rule: multiple separate versions are not allowed unless they are the single approved workspace version.

source_story: As a Web Developer, I want to be able to install multiple versions of the same datapackage separately, so that that all my projects could be developed independently and deployed locally.
rewritten_story: As a Web Developer, I want to be able to install multiple versions of the same datapackage separately for projects deployed remotely, so that all my projects can be developed independently for hosted environments.
rationale: The generated story keeps the same capability of separately installing multiple versions of the same datapackage, but changes the central deployment condition from local deployment to remote deployment, making the two stories materially incompatible in the runtime environment they target.

source_story: As a Web Developer, I want to be able to install multiple versions of the same datapackage separately, so that that all my projects could be developed independently and deployed locally.
rewritten_story: As a Web Developer, I want each project to declare its required datapackage name and version in a local dependency file, so that the package installer can resolve the correct datapackage for that project.
rationale: The generated story establishes the project-level version information needed before the system can install separate versions of the same datapackage for independent local projects.

source_story: As a Developer, I want to list all DataPackages requirements for my project in the file and pin the exact versions of any DataPackage that my project depends on, so that that the project can be deterministically deployed locally and won't break because of the DataPackage schema changes.
rewritten_story: As a Developer, I want to specify every DataPackages requirement for my project in the file and lock each dependent DataPackage to an exact version, so that the project can be deployed locally in a deterministic way and is not broken by DataPackage schema changes.
rationale: The rewritten story keeps the same role, requirement to record all DataPackages requirements and exact dependency versions, and the same benefit of deterministic local deployment without breakage from schema changes, while using different wording.

source_story: As a Developer, I want to list all DataPackages requirements for my project in the file and pin the exact versions of any DataPackage that my project depends on, so that that the project can be deterministically deployed locally and won't break because of the DataPackage schema changes.
rewritten_story: As a Developer, I want the DataPackages requirements file for my project to be editable only by project maintainers after review, so that dependency versions cannot be pinned or changed without ownership approval.
rationale: The generated story keeps the same DataPackages requirements and version-pinning feature area, but introduces an incompatible ownership and approval rule that prevents developers from directly listing or changing pinned dependencies.

source_story: As a Developer, I want to list all DataPackages requirements for my project in the file and pin the exact versions of any DataPackage that my project depends on, so that that the project can be deterministically deployed locally and won't break because of the DataPackage schema changes.
rewritten_story: As a Developer, I want to list all DataPackages requirements for my project in the file and pin the exact versions only when the dependency set is locked, so that the project can be deterministically deployed locally and won't break because of schema changes.
rationale: The generated story keeps the same dependency-management capability but changes the dependency-state condition under which version pinning applies, from a general case to the locked-dependency case, creating a materially different applicability condition.

source_story: As a Developer, I want to list all DataPackages requirements for my project in the file and pin the exact versions of any DataPackage that my project depends on, so that that the project can be deterministically deployed locally and won't break because of the DataPackage schema changes.
rewritten_story: As a Developer, I want to view the available DataPackages with their published version history and schema details, so that I can identify the exact DataPackage versions my project depends on.
rationale: The generated story establishes the necessary availability of DataPackage version and schema information before a developer can accurately list and pin exact DataPackage requirements for deterministic deployment.

source_story: As a Publisher, I want to be able to get access to a previous version I tagged, so that that I can return to it and review it.
rewritten_story: As a Publisher, I want to access an earlier version that I tagged so that I can go back to it and review it.
rationale: The rewritten story preserves the same role, objective, target version, and review benefit while expressing the requirement with different wording.

source_story: As a Publisher, I want to be able to get access to a previous version I tagged, so that that I can return to it and review it.
rewritten_story: As a Managing Editor, I want publishers to be unable to access previous versions they tagged unless a managing editor approves the request, so that archived content remains controlled.
rationale: The generated story keeps the same version-access feature area but changes the governing access rule by requiring managing-editor approval before a publisher can access a tagged previous version.

source_story: As a Publisher, I want to be able to get access to a previous version I tagged, so that that I can return to it and review it.
rewritten_story: As a Publisher, I want to be able to get access to a previous version that is untagged, so that that I can return to it and review it.
rationale: The generated story keeps the same role, capability, target artifact, and review goal, but changes the central version condition from tagged to untagged, making the two stories materially incompatible as stated.

source_story: As a Publisher, I want to be able to get access to a previous version I tagged, so that that I can return to it and review it.
rewritten_story: As a Publisher, I want to tag a saved version of my content, so that it can be identified later when I need to return to it for review.
rationale: A publisher must first be able to tag a saved version before they can later access a previous version they tagged.

source_story: As a Consumer, I want to get a sense of the age of the datapackage that I have downloaded before, so that that I can decide if I should update or not.
rewritten_story: As a Consumer, I want to see how old a datapackage I previously downloaded is, so that I can determine whether I should update it.
rationale: The rewritten story keeps the same role, objective, target datapackage, and update-decision benefit while expressing the requirement in different wording.

source_story: As a Consumer, I want to get a sense of the age of the datapackage that I have downloaded before, so that that I can decide if I should update or not.
rewritten_story: As a data owner, I want the age of a previously downloaded datapackage to be visible only to package owners and curators, so that consumers cannot access version-age information unless it is explicitly shared.
rationale: The generated story keeps the same datapackage age and update-decision context but changes the access-control rule so consumers are not allowed to view the age information directly.

source_story: As a Consumer, I want to get a sense of the age of the datapackage that I have downloaded before, so that that I can decide if I should update or not.
rewritten_story: As a Consumer, I want to get a sense of the age of a datapackage that I have not downloaded before, so that I can decide whether it is current enough to use.
rationale: The generated story keeps the same datapackage age-awareness capability but changes the central condition from a datapackage the Consumer has downloaded before to one they have not downloaded before, making the two stories incompatible while staying in the same backlog area.

source_story: As a Consumer, I want to get a sense of the age of the datapackage that I have downloaded before, so that that I can decide if I should update or not.
rewritten_story: As a Consumer, I want the system to retain the download date and version information for datapackages I have downloaded, so that their age can be determined later.
rationale: The generated story establishes the necessary prior data about a previously downloaded datapackage that must exist before the consumer can assess its age and decide whether to update.

source_story: As a Consumer, I want to see how active the site is, so that I can decide if I should get involved.
rewritten_story: As a Consumer, I want to view the site's level of activity, so that I can determine whether to participate.
rationale: The rewritten story keeps the same role, objective, target, and benefit while expressing the requirement in different wording.

source_story: As a Consumer, I want to see how active the site is, so that I can decide if I should get involved.
rewritten_story: As a site administrator, I want site activity metrics to be visible only to staff, so that internal engagement data is not exposed to consumers deciding whether to join.
rationale: The generated story keeps the same site activity visibility topic but introduces an incompatible access-control rule that prevents consumers from seeing the activity information described in the source story.

source_story: As a Consumer, I want to see how active the site is, so that I can decide if I should get involved.
rewritten_story: As a Consumer, I want to see how inactive the site is, so that I can decide if I should get involved.
rationale: The generated story keeps the same consumer decision-making goal and site activity context, but changes the central condition being assessed from site activity to site inactivity, making the stated focus materially incompatible.

source_story: As a Consumer, I want to see how active the site is, so that I can decide if I should get involved.
rewritten_story: As a site administrator, I want the system to collect recent participation data such as posts, comments, and member activity so that site activity can be shown to consumers.
rationale: The generated story establishes the necessary activity data that must exist before consumers can view how active the site is.

source_story: As an Owner, I want to edit my profile, so that that it is updated with new information.
rewritten_story: As an Owner, I want to update my profile so that it reflects my new information.
rationale: The rewritten story keeps the same role, objective, and benefit while expressing the requirement in different wording.

source_story: As an Owner, I want to edit my profile, so that that it is updated with new information.
rewritten_story: As an Owner, I want my profile details to be editable only by an account administrator, so that changes to owner information are controlled.
rationale: The generated story keeps the profile-update feature area but changes the permission rule so the Owner cannot edit their own profile directly, making it materially incompatible with the source story.

source_story: As an Owner, I want to edit my profile, so that that it is updated with new information.
rewritten_story: As an Owner, I want to edit my profile only while it is private, so that I can update it before making it visible to others.
rationale: The generated story keeps the same profile-editing capability but changes the profile visibility state under which editing applies, from a general case to the private-profile case, creating a materially different applicability condition.

source_story: As an Owner, I want to edit my profile, so that that it is updated with new information.
rewritten_story: As an Owner, I want to create my profile with my basic information, so that there is an existing profile available for me to update later.
rationale: The generated story establishes the necessary prior condition that an Owner must have an existing profile before they can edit it with new information.

source_story: As an owner, I want to invite someone using their email to sign up and become a member of my Publisher, so that that they are authorized to publish data packages under my Publisher.
rewritten_story: As an owner, I want to send an email invitation for someone to register and join my Publisher as a member, so that they have permission to publish data packages under my Publisher.
rationale: The rewritten story preserves the same role, invitation-by-email workflow, membership objective, and publishing authorization benefit while expressing the requirement in different wording.

source_story: As an owner, I want to invite someone using their email to sign up and become a member of my Publisher, so that that they are authorized to publish data packages under my Publisher.
rewritten_story: As an owner, I want email invitations to require approval from a Publisher administrator before the invited person becomes a member authorized to publish data packages under my Publisher.
rationale: The generated story keeps the same invitation and Publisher membership workflow but changes the governing approval rule so an owner invitation alone is not sufficient to grant publishing authorization.

source_story: As an owner, I want to invite someone using their email to sign up and become a member of my Publisher, so that that they are authorized to publish data packages under my Publisher.
rewritten_story: As an owner, I want to invite someone who already has an active account using their email to become a member of my Publisher, so that they are authorized to publish data packages under my Publisher.
rationale: The generated story keeps the same Publisher membership invitation capability but changes the recipient condition from needing to sign up to already having an active account, making the two stories materially incompatible in when the invitation flow applies.

source_story: As an owner, I want to invite someone using their email to sign up and become a member of my Publisher, so that that they are authorized to publish data packages under my Publisher.
rewritten_story: As an owner, I want to register my Publisher and be recognized as its owner, so that I can manage membership for that Publisher.
rationale: The generated story establishes the necessary prior state that a Publisher exists and the user has owner rights before they can invite someone to become a member and publish under it.

source_story: As an Admin, I want to set key configuration parameters for my site deployment, so that that I can change key information like the site title.
rewritten_story: As an Admin, I want to configure important settings for my site deployment, so that I can update key details such as the site title.
rationale: The rewritten story keeps the same Admin role, site deployment configuration objective, and benefit of changing key information like the site title while using different wording.

source_story: As an Admin, I want to set key configuration parameters for my site deployment, so that that I can change key information like the site title.
rewritten_story: As an Admin, I want key configuration parameters for my site deployment, such as the site title, to be editable only by deployment owners so that administrative users cannot make unauthorized site-level changes.
rationale: The generated story keeps the same configuration area but changes the governing access rule by preventing Admins from editing key deployment parameters and reserving that permission for deployment owners.

source_story: As an Admin, I want to set key configuration parameters for my site deployment, so that that I can change key information like the site title.
rewritten_story: As an Admin, I want to set key configuration parameters for my site deployment only while the site is offline, so that I can change key information before it is visible to visitors.
rationale: The generated story keeps the same configuration capability but changes the site runtime state under which configuration changes apply, from a general case to the offline-site case, creating a materially different applicability condition.

source_story: As an Admin, I want to set key configuration parameters for my site deployment, so that that I can change key information like the site title.
rewritten_story: As an Admin, I want to create a site deployment with an initial configuration profile, so that key settings are available for me to manage later.
rationale: The generated story establishes the required prior system state—a site deployment with configurable settings—needed before an Admin can change key configuration parameters such as the site title.

source_story: As an Admin, I want to see key metrics about usage such as users, API usage, downloads, so that that I know how things are going.
rewritten_story: As an Admin, I want to view important usage metrics, such as users, API usage, and downloads, so that I can understand how things are going.
rationale: The rewritten story keeps the same role, usage metrics, examples, and intended benefit while expressing the requirement with different wording.

source_story: As an Admin, I want to see key metrics about usage such as users, API usage, downloads, so that that I know how things are going.
rewritten_story: As an Admin, I want key usage metrics such as users, API usage, and downloads to be viewable only by organization owners, so that sensitive usage information is not exposed to general admins.
rationale: The generated story keeps the same usage-metrics feature area but changes the access-control rule so that general admins are restricted from viewing metrics that the source story allows them to see.

source_story: As an Admin, I want to see key metrics about usage such as users, API usage, downloads, so that that I know how things are going.
rewritten_story: As an Admin, I want to see key metrics about usage such as users, API usage, and downloads for archived reporting periods, so that I know how things went.
rationale: The generated story keeps the same admin metrics dashboard capability and usage metrics, but changes the central condition from current ongoing usage to archived reporting periods, making the two reporting contexts materially incompatible.

source_story: As an Admin, I want to see key metrics about usage such as users, API usage, downloads, so that that I know how things are going.
rewritten_story: As an Admin, I want user activity, API calls, and downloads to be captured as usage data so that key usage metrics can be reported accurately.
rationale: The generated story establishes the necessary availability of captured usage data before the admin can view metrics about users, API usage, and downloads.

source_story: As an owner, I want to view all the people in my organization and what roles they have, so that that I can change these if I want.
rewritten_story: As an owner, I want to see everyone in my organization along with the roles assigned to them, so that I can modify those role assignments when I choose.
rationale: The rewritten story keeps the same role, objective, target information, and intended benefit while expressing the requirement in different wording.

source_story: As a Publisher, I want to permanently delete a data package, so that that it no longer takes up storage space.
rewritten_story: As a Publisher, I want to remove a data package for good so that it no longer uses storage space.
rationale: The rewritten story keeps the same role, action, target object, and storage-space benefit while expressing the requirement with different wording.

source_story: As a Publisher, I want to publish a dataset, so that I can share the dataset publicly with everyone.
rewritten_story: As a Publisher, I want to make a dataset available publicly so that everyone can access and use it.
rationale: The rewritten story keeps the same role, dataset publishing objective, and public sharing benefit while expressing the requirement in different wording.

source_story: As a Publisher, I want to be able to preview the views of the current data package using cli prior to publishing, so that that I can refine the json declarations of datapackage view section to achieve a great looking result.
rewritten_story: As a Publisher, I want to use the CLI to preview the current data package views before publishing, so that I can adjust the JSON declarations in the datapackage view section and produce a polished result.
rationale: The rewritten story keeps the same role, CLI-based preview capability, pre-publishing context, and purpose of refining the datapackage view JSON declarations while using different wording.

source_story: As a Publisher, I want to be able to restore the deleted data package via cli, so that that it is back visible and available to view, download.
rewritten_story: As a Publisher, I want to restore a deleted data package using the CLI so that it is visible again and available for viewing and downloading.
rationale: The rewritten story keeps the same role, objective, target data package, CLI access method, and outcome while expressing the requirement in cleaner alternative wording.

source_story: As a Publisher, I want to create a data package in the UI so that it is available and published.
rewritten_story: As a Publisher, I want to use the UI to create a data package so that it becomes available and published.
rationale: The rewritten story keeps the same role, objective, interface context, and outcome while expressing the requirement in different wording.

source_story: As a Publisher, I want to validate the data I am about to publish to the registry, so that that I publish good data and know that I am doing.
rewritten_story: As a Publisher, I want to check the data before I publish it to the registry, so that I can publish good data and understand what I am doing.
rationale: The rewritten story keeps the same role, action, target data, registry context, and intended benefit while expressing the requirement in different wording.

source_story: As a Publisher, I want to unpublish a data package, so that it is no longer visible to anyone.
rewritten_story: As a Publisher, I want to remove a data package from publication so that no one can see it anymore.
rationale: The rewritten story keeps the same role, action, target object, and visibility outcome while expressing the requirement in different wording.

source_story: As a Consumer, I want to be notified of changes to a package I care about, so that that I can check out what has changed and take action.
rewritten_story: As a Consumer, I want to receive alerts when a package I care about changes, so that I can review the updates and decide what action to take.
rationale: The rewritten story keeps the same role, package-change notification need, and intended benefit while expressing the requirement in different wording.

source_story: As a Publisher, I want to use a publish command to update a data package that is already in the registry, so that it appears there.
rewritten_story: As a Publisher, I want to update an existing data package in the registry by running a publish command, so that it is displayed there.
rationale: The rewritten story keeps the same role, action, target data package, registry context, and intended visibility outcome while using different wording.

source_story: As a Consumer, I want to view a publisher's profile, so that that I can see who is behind a particular package or to see what other packages they produce.
rewritten_story: As a Consumer, I want to open a publisher's profile so that I can identify who is responsible for a specific package and browse the other packages they publish.
rationale: The rewritten story keeps the same role, objective, target profile, and benefit while expressing the requirement in different wording.

source_story: As a Publisher, I want to tag datapackage to create a snapshot of data on the registry server, so that that consumers can refer to it.
rewritten_story: As a Publisher, I want to apply a tag to a datapackage to capture a snapshot of the data on the registry server, so that consumers can reference it.
rationale: The rewritten story keeps the same role, action, target object, registry-server snapshot purpose, and consumer reference benefit while expressing the requirement in different wording.

source_story: As a Publisher, I want to sign up for an account, so that that I can publish my data package to the registry and to have a publisher account to publish my data package under.
rewritten_story: As a Publisher, I want to create an account so that I can submit my data package to the registry and publish it under my publisher account.
rationale: The rewritten story keeps the same role, account sign-up objective, data package publishing target, and publisher-account benefit while expressing the requirement in different wording.

source_story: As a Publisher, I want to sign up for a given pricing plan, so that that I am entitled to what it allows.
rewritten_story: As a Publisher, I want to enroll in a specific pricing plan so that I can access the benefits included with that plan.
rationale: The rewritten story keeps the same role, objective of joining a pricing plan, and benefit of receiving what the plan permits, while expressing the requirement in different wording.

source_story: As an Owner, I want to invite an existing user, so that the user can become a member of my publisher.
rewritten_story: As an Owner, I want to send an invitation to a current user so that they can join my publisher as a member.
rationale: The rewritten story keeps the same role, action, target user, and intended outcome while expressing the requirement with different wording.

source_story: As a Publisher, I want to publish a data package in the UI, so that that it is available and published.
rewritten_story: As a Publisher, I want to use the UI to publish a data package so that it becomes published and available.
rationale: The rewritten story preserves the same role, action, target object, UI context, and intended outcome while expressing the requirement with different wording.

source_story: As an Admin, I want to have a pricing plan and billing system, so that that I can charge users and make my platform sustainable.
rewritten_story: As an Admin, I want a pricing plan with a billing system so that I can collect payments from users and keep my platform sustainable.
rationale: The rewritten story preserves the Admin role, the need for pricing and billing, and the benefit of charging users to sustain the platform while using different wording.

source_story: As a Publisher, I want to undelete the deleted data packages, so that that the deleted data packages is now visible again.
rewritten_story: As a Publisher, I want to restore deleted data packages so that they are visible again.
rationale: The rewritten story keeps the same role, objective of undeleting/restoring deleted data packages, and benefit of making them visible again while using clearer alternative wording.

source_story: As a publisher, I want to show the world how my published data is, so that that it immediately catches consumer's attention.
rewritten_story: As a publisher, I want to present my published data to the world so that it quickly attracts consumers' attention.
rationale: The rewritten story keeps the same publisher role, objective of showing published data publicly, and benefit of immediately drawing consumer attention while using different wording.

source_story: As a Publisher, I want to publish a dataset, so that I can view just the dataset with a few people.
rewritten_story: As a Publisher, I want to release a dataset, so that I can view that specific dataset with a small group of people.
rationale: The rewritten story keeps the same role, intent to publish a dataset, and benefit of viewing only that dataset with a few people, while using different wording.

source_story: As an owner, I want to view all the people in my organization and what roles they have, so that that I can change these if I want.
rewritten_story: As an owner, I want to view all the people in my organization and their roles, but require role changes to be approved by another owner before they take effect.
rationale: The generated story keeps the same organization role-management feature area but changes the governing approval rule, making role changes no longer something an owner can apply independently whenever they want.

source_story: As a Publisher, I want to permanently delete a data package, so that that it no longer takes up storage space.
rewritten_story: As a Publisher, I want permanent deletion of a data package to be restricted to repository administrators after approval from the package owner, so that publishers cannot remove stored packages without oversight.
rationale: The generated story keeps the same data package deletion feature area but changes the governing access and approval rule, making it incompatible with publishers directly permanently deleting packages.

source_story: As a Publisher, I want to publish a dataset, so that I can share the dataset publicly with everyone.
rewritten_story: As a Publisher, I want public dataset publishing to be allowed only after a Data Steward approves the dataset, so that public sharing is controlled before release.
rationale: The generated story keeps the same dataset publishing feature area but introduces an approval rule that is materially incompatible with the source story's direct public publishing permission.

source_story: As a Publisher, I want to be able to preview the views of the current data package using cli prior to publishing, so that that I can refine the json declarations of datapackage view section to achieve a great looking result.
rewritten_story: As a platform administrator, I want CLI previews of the current data package views before publishing to be available only to package administrators, so that publishers cannot access unpublished view renderings without elevated permission.
rationale: The generated story keeps the same CLI preview feature for unpublished data package views but changes the access-control rule so publishers are not allowed to preview them, making it materially incompatible with the source story.

source_story: As a Publisher, I want to be able to restore the deleted data package via cli, so that that it is back visible and available to view, download.
rewritten_story: As a Publisher, I want deleted data packages to be restorable via cli only after an administrator approves the restore request, so that packages are not made visible and downloadable again without governance review.
rationale: The generated story keeps the same CLI restore workflow for deleted data packages but changes the governing rule from a publisher being able to restore directly to requiring administrator approval, making the permission policy materially incompatible.

source_story: As a Publisher, I want to create a data package in the UI so that it is available and published.
rewritten_story: As a Publisher, I want data packages I create in the UI to remain in draft status until a Data Steward approves them for publication, so that only reviewed packages become available.
rationale: The generated story keeps the same data-package creation and publishing workflow but introduces an approval rule that prevents Publishers from making UI-created packages immediately available and published.

source_story: As a Publisher, I want to validate the data I am about to publish to the registry, so that that I publish good data and know that I am doing.
rewritten_story: As a Publisher, I want data validation before publishing to the registry to require approval from an independent registry reviewer, so that publishers cannot validate and approve their own submissions.
rationale: The generated story keeps the same registry data validation topic but introduces an incompatible approval rule: publishers may no longer self-validate their own data before publication.

source_story: As a Publisher, I want to unpublish a data package, so that it is no longer visible to anyone.
rewritten_story: As a Publisher, I want data packages to be unpublished only by repository administrators, so that publishers cannot remove visible data packages without authorized control.
rationale: The generated story keeps the same data package unpublishing feature area but changes the permission rule so publishers are not allowed to unpublish directly, which is materially incompatible with the source story.

source_story: As a Consumer, I want to be notified of changes to a package I care about, so that that I can check out what has changed and take action.
rewritten_story: As a package owner, I want consumers to receive change notifications for my package only after I approve their watch request, so that I can control who is alerted about package updates.
rationale: The generated story keeps the same package-change notification area but introduces an approval and ownership rule that is incompatible with consumers freely receiving notifications for any package they care about.

source_story: As a Publisher, I want to use a publish command to update a data package that is already in the registry, so that it appears there.
rewritten_story: As a registry administrator, I want the publish command to refuse updates to data packages that are already in the registry unless a registry maintainer has approved the change, so that existing packages are not changed without review.
rationale: The generated story keeps the same publish-command and registry update context but changes the governing rule from publisher-driven updates to maintainer-approved updates, making the permission policy materially incompatible.

source_story: As a Consumer, I want to view a publisher's profile, so that that I can see who is behind a particular package or to see what other packages they produce.
rewritten_story: As a Consumer, I want to view a publisher's profile only when the profile is public, so that I can see who is behind a particular package or what other packages they produce.
rationale: The generated story keeps the same consumer goal and publisher-profile feature area while making the viewing capability apply specifically to the public state of the profile, creating a clear state-based condition for when the story applies.

source_story: As a Publisher, I want to tag datapackage to create a snapshot of data on the registry server, so that that consumers can refer to it.
rewritten_story: As a Publisher, I want to tag a draft datapackage to create a snapshot of data on the registry server, so that consumers can refer to the draft version.
rationale: The generated story keeps the tagging and registry snapshot capability but changes the central datapackage state to draft, making the intended snapshot apply to a different lifecycle condition than a general consumable package.

source_story: As a Publisher, I want to sign up for an account, so that that I can publish my data package to the registry and to have a publisher account to publish my data package under.
rewritten_story: As a Publisher with an existing active account, I want to publish my data package to the registry under that account, so that my package is associated with my publisher profile.
rationale: The generated story stays in the publisher account and registry publishing workflow but changes the central account state from needing to sign up for a publisher account to already having an active account, making the stated conditions incompatible.

source_story: As a Publisher, I want to sign up for a given pricing plan, so that that I am entitled to what it allows.
rewritten_story: As a Publisher, I want to sign up for a given inactive pricing plan, so that I am entitled to what it allows if that plan is not currently offered.
rationale: The generated story keeps the same signup capability and pricing-plan subject, but changes the central plan condition to inactive rather than the normally available plan implied by signup, creating a materially different state under which the capability applies.

source_story: As an Owner, I want to invite an existing user, so that the user can become a member of my publisher.
rewritten_story: As an Owner, I want to invite a user who does not yet have an account, so that the user can become a member of my publisher after joining.
rationale: The generated story keeps the same owner invitation capability and publisher membership goal, but changes the central user condition from already existing in the system to not yet having an account, making the two invitation cases mutually incompatible.

source_story: As a Publisher, I want to publish a data package in the UI, so that that it is available and published.
rewritten_story: As a Publisher, I want to publish a data package in the UI as private, so that it is published but not publicly available.
rationale: The generated story keeps the same publisher role, UI workflow, and data package publishing capability, but changes the central availability state from publicly available to private/not publicly available, making the two stories materially incompatible.

source_story: As an Admin, I want to have a pricing plan and billing system, so that that I can charge users and make my platform sustainable.
rewritten_story: As an Admin, I want to have a pricing plan and billing system for users with expired subscriptions, so that I can charge them for renewal and keep my platform sustainable.
rationale: The generated story keeps the same pricing and billing feature area but changes the central billing condition to users whose subscriptions are expired, making the applicable subscription state materially different from a general active billing setup.

source_story: As a Publisher, I want to undelete the deleted data packages, so that that the deleted data packages is now visible again.
rewritten_story: As a Publisher, I want to undelete the deleted data packages so that the restored data packages remain hidden from public listings.
rationale: The generated story keeps the same publisher action and target object, but changes the resulting package state from visible again to hidden after restoration, making the two requirements materially incompatible.

source_story: As a publisher, I want to show the world how my published data is, so that that it immediately catches consumer's attention.
rewritten_story: As a publisher, I want to show the world how my draft data will look, so that it can catch consumers' attention before it is published.
rationale: The generated story keeps the same publisher goal of presenting data to attract consumer attention, but changes the central data state from published to draft, making the applicable condition materially incompatible with the source.

source_story: As a Publisher, I want to publish a dataset, so that I can view just the dataset with a few people.
rewritten_story: As a Publisher, I want to publish a dataset as public, so that anyone can view it.
rationale: The generated story keeps the same publishing capability and dataset target but changes the dataset visibility condition from limited to a few people to public, making the two stories materially incompatible.
